agent_output:
# Domain Analysis: Smart Route Optimization

## Domain Analysis

### Business Context

This feature encodes the **operational dispatch and route planning process** for a last-mile delivery logistics platform. It sits at the intersection of three core business concerns: (1) efficiently assigning work to drivers, (2) dynamically responding to real-world delivery conditions, and (3) maintaining regulatory/operational compliance around vehicle-delivery matching. This is not merely a routing algorithm — it is the encoding of how a logistics company orchestrates its most expensive resource (drivers + vehicles) against its most time-sensitive obligation (delivery promises to customers).

### Bounded Contexts Identified

I identify **four distinct bounded contexts** that this feature touches. Getting these boundaries wrong will cause domain drift and tangled responsibilities.

---

**1. Route Planning Context**
Owns: route calculation, stop ordering, recalculation triggers, time estimation.

**2. Fleet & Driver Management Context**
Owns: driver shifts, vehicle assignments, vehicle class licensing, zone assignments.

**3. Delivery Context**
Owns: delivery lifecycle (pending → assigned → in-transit → delivered/failed), delivery attributes (size, weight, type), zone membership.

**4. Customer Notification Context**
Owns: notification triggers, delivery proximity tracking, customer communication preferences.

---

### Ubiquitous Language

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Zone** | A geographic partition of the service area used to scope driver assignments and route calculation. A delivery belongs to exactly one zone. A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift. |
| **Shift** | A bounded work period during which a driver is active and eligible for route assignment. A shift has a start time, end time, and a single associated zone. |
| **Optimized Route** | An ordered sequence of delivery stops calculated to minimize total drive time/distance within a driver's shift and zone. It is NOT a suggestion — it is the system's prescribed plan of execution. |
| **Recalculation Trigger** | A domain event that invalidates the current optimized route and requires a new route to be computed. Three triggers exist: new delivery added, delivery marked failed, driver deviation. |
| **Vehicle Class** | A classification of vehicle capability (e.g., motorcycle, van, truck, freight). Each vehicle class defines the set of delivery types it can legally and physically fulfill. |
| **Licensed Vehicle Class** | The vehicle class a driver is currently operating under for their shift. This is a property of the driver-vehicle assignment, NOT the driver themselves (a driver may hold multiple licenses but operates one vehicle per shift). |
| **Delivery Stop** | A single delivery location within a route. Has an estimated service time (default 4 minutes, overridable per zone). |
| **Next-3-Stops Window** | The set of the next 3 upcoming delivery stops on the driver's active route. Entry into this window triggers customer notification. This is a sliding window that advances as stops are completed. |
| **Driver Deviation** | When a driver's actual GPS position or stop sequence diverges from the prescribed optimized route beyond a system-defined threshold. |
| **Failed Delivery** | A delivery that could not be completed (customer unavailable, access issue, refused, etc.). Removes the stop from the active route and triggers recalculation. |
| **Stop Service Time** | The estimated time a driver spends at a delivery stop (default: 4 minutes). Overridable by the operations team at the zone level. |
| **Pending Delivery** | A delivery that has been created and assigned to a zone but not yet assigned to a driver's route. |

---

## Business Rules

### Route Planning Context

| # | Rule | Invariant? | Testable Assertion |
|---|---|---|---|
| RP-1 | When a driver starts their shift, ALL pending deliveries in their zone MUST be included in the initial route calculation. | **Yes** | Given N pending deliveries in zone X, when a driver starts a shift in zone X, then the generated route contains exactly N stops (minus any that violate vehicle class). |
| RP-2 | A route recalculation MUST be triggered when a new delivery is added to the driver's active zone. | **Yes** | Given a driver with an active route in zone X, when a new delivery is created in zone X, then a recalculation event is emitted and a new route is produced. |
| RP-3 | A route recalculation MUST be triggered when a delivery on the active route is marked as failed. | **Yes** | Given a driver with stop D on their route, when D is marked failed, then D is removed and the remaining stops are re-optimized. |
| RP-4 | A route recalculation MUST be triggered when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route. | **Yes** | Given a driver on route R, when deviation is detected, then a new route is calculated from the driver's current position with remaining undelivered stops. |
| RP-5 | The default stop service time is 4 minutes. This value CAN be overridden by the operations team at the zone level. | No (soft) | Given zone X with no override, stop time = 4 min. Given zone X with override = 6 min, stop time = 6 min. |
| RP-6 | A recalculation MUST preserve already-completed stops — it only re-optimizes remaining stops. | **Yes** | Given a route with 10 stops where 4 are completed, when recalculation fires, then the new route contains only the 6 remaining stops. |

### Fleet & Driver Management Context

| # | Rule | Invariant? | Testable Assertion |
|---|---|---|---|
| FD-1 | A driver MUST NOT be assigned a delivery that exceeds their licensed vehicle class capability. | **Yes** | Given a motorcycle driver, when a freight delivery exists in their zone, then that delivery is EXCLUDED from their route. |
| FD-2 | Vehicle class compatibility is checked at assignment time AND at recalculation time. | **Yes** | Given a motorcycle driver with an active route, when a new freight delivery is added to their zone, then that delivery is NOT added to the recalculated route. |
| FD-3 | A driver operates exactly one vehicle class per shift. | **Yes** | Given driver D starting shift S with vehicle V (class: van), then all route assignments during S use vehicle class = van. |

### Customer Notification Context

| # | Rule | Invariant? | Testable Assertion |
|---|---|---|---|
| CN-1 | A customer MUST be notified when their delivery enters the next-3-stops window. | **Yes** | Given a route with stops [A, B, C, D, E], when driver completes stop A (or hasn't started), then customers for B, C, D receive notifications. |
| CN-2 | A customer MUST NOT receive duplicate "approaching" notifications for the same delivery. | **Yes** | Given customer C already notified for delivery D, when route recalculation places D still within the next-3 window, then no duplicate notification is sent. |
| CN-3 | If a recalculation moves a delivery OUT of the next-3-stops window, the customer's expectation must be managed. | No (soft) | Given customer C was notified, when recalculation moves their delivery from position 3 to position 7, then a revised ETA notification should be sent. |

---

## Acceptance Criteria

### AC-1: Shift Start Route Generation
- **Given** a driver starts a shift in zone "Downtown", and there are 12 pending deliveries in "Downtown" (10 van-compatible, 2 freight-only), and the driver's vehicle class is "van"
- **When** the shift begins
- **Then** the system generates an optimized route with exactly 10 stops (the 2 freight deliveries are excluded), ordered by optimization algorithm, with ETAs based on zone stop service time

### AC-2: New Delivery Triggers Recalculation
- **Given** a driver has an active route with 8 remaining stops in zone "Downtown"
- **When** a new van-compatible delivery is added to zone "Downtown"
- **Then** the route is recalculated to include 9 stops, the 3 already-completed stops are not affected, and customers in the new next-3 window are notified

### AC-3: Failed Delivery Triggers Recalculation
- **Given** a driver is en route to stop 4 of 10
- **When** stop 4 is marked as "failed"
- **Then** stop 4 is removed, the route is recalculated with 6 remaining stops (5-10 minus failed), and ETAs for subsequent stops are updated

### AC-4: Driver Deviation Triggers Recalculation
- **Given** a driver's prescribed next stop is location A but GPS shows them heading toward location C
- **When** the system detects deviation beyond threshold
- **Then** a new route is calculated from the driver's current position including all remaining undelivered stops

### AC-5: Vehicle Class Enforcement
- **Given** a motorcycle driver in zone "Suburbs"
- **When** a freight delivery is added to zone "Suburbs"
- **Then** the freight delivery is NOT assigned to the motorcycle driver's route, and it remains in "pending" status awaiting a compatible driver

### AC-6: Customer Notification Window
- **Given** a driver's route is [A, B, C, D, E, F] and the driver just completed stop A
- **When** the next-3 window becomes [B, C, D]
- **Then** customers for B, C, and D receive a notification with their estimated delivery time

### AC-7: Zone-Level Service Time Override
- **Given** the operations team sets zone "Airport" stop service time to 8 minutes
- **When** a route is calculated for a driver in zone "Airport" with 5 stops
- **Then** the total estimated service time reflects 8 minutes per stop (not the default 4)

### Edge Cases

| Scenario | Expected Behavior |
|---|---|
| **Zero pending deliveries at shift start** | Driver is assigned an empty route and enters an "awaiting deliveries" state. Route is generated upon first delivery arrival. |
| **All deliveries in zone exceed driver's vehicle class** | Same as zero — empty route, awaiting compatible deliveries. The incompatible deliveries must NOT be force-assigned. |
| **Rapid successive recalculation triggers** (3 new deliveries added within seconds) | System must debounce or batch recalculations to avoid thrashing. Each recalculation must be atomic — no partial route states visible to driver. |
| **Driver deviates to a stop that IS on their route, just out of order** | System should recognize the stop was completed (not truly deviated) and recalculate remaining stops from that point. Do not treat a legitimate out-of-order completion as an error. |
| **Delivery added to zone with multiple active drivers** | System must decide WHICH driver gets the delivery. This is an assignment problem the product spec does NOT address — see escalation below. |
| **Route recalculation while driver is mid-delivery at a stop** | Recalculation must not interrupt the current stop. New route applies AFTER current stop is completed. |
| **Customer notified, then route recalculation delays their delivery significantly** | Must send an updated ETA notification (CN-3). Failing to do so violates customer trust even if not a hard system invariant. |
| **Shift ends before all stops completed** | Undelivered stops must return to "pending" status and be eligible for reassignment. They must NOT remain orphaned on an expired route. |

---

## Business Impact

| Dimension | Assessment |
|---|---|
| **Financial** | High impact. Route optimization directly affects fuel costs, deliveries-per-shift throughput, and driver labor utilization. A 10% improvement in route efficiency could reduce per-delivery cost significantly at scale. Vehicle class enforcement prevents costly mis-deliveries (wrong vehicle = failed delivery = second attempt = doubled cost). |
| **Operational** | High impact. Dynamic recalculation reduces dispatcher workload (previously manual re-routing). Zone-level service time overrides give ops teams a tuning lever for areas with known access delays (airports, gated communities). However, excessive recalculation could create driver fatigue/confusion if not debounced. |
| **User — Driver** | Moderate-to-high impact. Drivers gain clear guidance and reduced decision-making. Risk: frequent recalculations mid-shift may frustrate experienced drivers. The "deviation" trigger must have a reasonable threshold — too sensitive and it fires on legal U-turns; too loose and it defeats the purpose. |
| **User — Customer** | High impact. The next-3-stops notification is a significant customer experience upgrade, providing transparency. However, if recalculations cause notification churn (notified → delayed → notified again), customer trust erodes. |
| **Risk — Compliance** | Vehicle class enforcement is likely a regulatory requirement (CDL laws, weight restrictions). Failure is not just a business problem — it's a legal liability. This rule is NON-NEGOTIABLE. |
| **Risk — Technical** | Recalculation under load (many drivers, many zones, frequent triggers) is a scalability concern. Route optimization is computationally expensive (NP-hard in general). The debounce/batching strategy is architecturally critical. |

### Stakeholders Affected

| Group | Impact |
|---|---|
| **Drivers** | Primary users. Route quality directly affects their workday efficiency and satisfaction. Poor routes = frustration, turnover risk. |
| **Customers** | Receive delivery and notifications. ETA accuracy builds or erodes trust. |
| **Operations Team** | Gains zone-level tuning controls. Reduced manual dispatch workload. Needs training on service time overrides. |
| **Dispatch/Planning** | Role shifts from manual routing to exception handling. May resist if perceived as job displacement. |
| **Finance** | Benefits from efficiency gains. Needs visibility into per-route cost metrics. |
| **Compliance/Legal** | Vehicle class enforcement must be audit-logged. Any violation is a liability event. |

---

## Confidence Assessment

| Attribute | Value |
|---|---|
| **Confidence Level** | **72%** |
| **High Confidence Areas** | Vehicle class invariant (clear, non-negotiable), recalculation triggers (well-specified), customer notification window (clear rule), stop service time override (straightforward) |
| **Low Confidence Areas** | Multi-driver assignment policy (spec is silent on how a new delivery is assigned when multiple drivers are active in a zone), deviation detection threshold (not defined), shift-end handoff behavior (not specified), recalculation debounce strategy (not specified) |
| **Assumptions** | (1) One driver per zone at a time, OR there is an existing assignment policy — spec is ambiguous. (2) "Optimized" means minimizing total travel time/distance — actual optimization objective is not stated. (3) Route deviation is GPS-based — detection mechanism is unspecified. (4) Notifications are push notifications to customers — channel is unspecified. (5) Zone boundaries are pre-configured and static during a shift. |

---

## Escalations to Product Team

> **ESCALATION 1 — Multi-driver zone assignment**: The spec says "assigns them an optimized route based on all pending deliveries in their zone." What happens when a SECOND driver starts a shift in the same zone? Are pending deliveries split between drivers? If a new delivery arrives mid-shift with two active drivers, who gets it? This is a critical assignment policy gap.

> **ESCALATION 2 — Optimization objective**: "Optimized route" is undefined. Minimize total distance? Minimize total time? Prioritize deliveries with tighter SLAs? Minimize fuel cost? The optimization objective fundamentally changes the route output. This must be explicitly defined.

> **ESCALATION 3 — Deviation threshold**: What constitutes "deviation"? Is it distance from prescribed path (>500m off-route)? Is it visiting stops out of order? Is it time-based (>10 min behind schedule)? Without a clear threshold, this trigger will either fire too often (driver frustration) or never fire (useless).

> **ESCALATION 4 — Shift-end behavior**: What happens to undelivered stops when a shift ends? Return to pending? Auto-assigned to next shift? This is critical for delivery SLA compliance and is completely absent from the spec.
